title: Rolling clay coils
subtitle: Making consistent coils for coil-built pottery
excerpt: Coil-building is one of the oldest ceramic methods: coils of clay are stacked and joined to raise a wall without a wheel. Consistent coil diameter is what separates a pot that dries evenly from one that develops stress cracks at the joins. This tutorial covers the palm-rolling technique used to produce coils of the same width along their full length.
sourceNotes: Coil-building is among the earliest documented pottery techniques. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry for construction methods. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rope of clay rolled to an even diameter, used as the building unit in coil-built pottery. Stacked and joined to raise a wall.
A hand-building method where coils of clay are stacked on a base and joined to build up the walls of a vessel.

content.text: A
coil

is a rope of clay that must be the same diameter throughout its length. Variation in diameter produces thin walls at wide points and thick walls at narrow points: the thin sections dry faster, pull away from the thick sections, and crack. Rolling consistent coils takes practice, but the method is straightforward. No kiln or wheel is needed.

attrs.title: Clay condition
attrs.body: Air-dry clay should be firm but pliable for coiling, not sticky. If it sticks to your palms and smears, leave it uncovered for 20 minutes before starting. If it crumbles at the edges, mist with water and leave for an hour.

content.text: What you need
The rolling method
Step 1: Cut a cylinder of clay

Cut a piece of clay approximately the diameter of a wine cork. Do not tear it from the bag: a clean cut from the wire cutter gives a cylinder with no hidden tension lines. The starting cylinder should be about 4 cm long and 3 cm across.
Step 2: Roll on a flat surface with both palms

Place the clay on the work surface. Lay both palms on top of the clay with fingers together. Apply light downward pressure and roll the clay forward and back under your palms. The pressure should come from the whole hand, not the fingertips. Fingertip pressure produces a coil that is thinner at the fingertip positions and thicker between them.
Step 3: Spread the pressure along the coil

As the coil lengthens, spread your hands apart so they cover the full length. Keep the rolling strokes even and the pressure constant. If one end is thicker than the other, apply slightly more pressure over the thick section on the next few passes. The target diameter for most coil-built work is 10 to 15 mm.
Step 4: Check for consistency

Hold the coil at each end and lift it off the board. A consistent coil hangs straight. A coil that bows or curves has uneven thickness: the clay on the inside of the curve is thicker. Lay it back down and roll more on the thick side.
What consistent coils look like

A correctly rolled coil is the same diameter from end to end and does not taper. The surface is smooth with no cracks along its length. When bent into a curve, the clay on the outside edge does not crack: cracking on the outer edge of a curved coil means the clay is too dry. When set down it holds the curve without springing back.
How many to make

Roll coils to order as you build: a coil left exposed to air starts to dry within 15 to 30 minutes and may crack when bent onto the pot. Make two or three coils at a time and cover the ones you are not using immediately with plastic. Only roll a fresh batch when you are ready to join the next layer.
Using coils in
coil-building

Score the surface the coil will sit on with a crosshatch of shallow lines, then apply a thin wash of clay slurry (water mixed with clay to the consistency of thick cream) before pressing the coil down. The scored surface and the slurry together give the join mechanical and chemical adhesion. Smooth the coil down on the inside wall with a finger or wooden rib after placing each layer.
Common problems

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems with coil consistency and what to do.

items.cause: The rolling stroke is longer at the ends where the palms separate.
items.fix: Stop the rolling stroke before the hands reach the ends. Keep both palms on the coil throughout the stroke.

items.symptom: Coil cracks along its length when bent
items.cause: The clay dried too much before bending.
items.fix: Work faster and keep unused coils covered. Slightly mist a cracked coil and work it gently: if it cracks again, discard and roll a fresh one.

items.symptom: Coil sticks to the work surface and smears
items.fix: Leave uncovered for 20 minutes, then try again. A lightly dusted surface with a pinch of dry clay powder also helps.
